Sophrosyne is the ability to actively be "tempted" towards good. It is linked to [[Phenomenological knowing]] and one's relationship with God

[[Category:John Vervaeke]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
[[sn:Sophrosyne]][[Category:Psychology]]
==Wikipedia==
{{Short description|Ancient Greek concept of an ideal of excellence of character and soundness of mind}}
{{about|the Greek virtue|the asteroid|134 Sophrosyne}}
[[Image:Edward Burne-Jones Temperantia 1872.jpg|thumb|right|''Temperantia'' (1872), by [[Edward Burne-Jones]]]]
'''Sophrosyne''' ({{lang-el|σωφροσύνη}}) is an [[Ancient Greece|ancient Greek]] concept of an [[Ideal (ethics)|ideal]] of [[Good moral character|excellence of character]] and [[Soundness|soundness]] of [[mind]], which when combined in one well-balanced individual leads to other qualities, such as [[Temperance (virtue)|temperance]], [[moderation]], [[prudence]], purity, [[decorum]], and [[self-control]]. An adjectival form is "sophron."<ref>[[Euripides]]. ''[[Hippolytus (play)|Hippolytos]]''. Oxford: Oxford University Press. 1973. {{ISBN|978-0-19-507290-7}}. [https://books.google.com/books?id=1-wp0-csU5QC&lpg=PP1&hl=de&pg=PA6 p. 6].</ref> 

It is similar to the concepts of ''[[Doctrine of the Mean|zhōngyōng]]'' (中庸) of Chinese [[Confucianism]]<ref>林之满 萧枫, ed. 2014. ''流光溢彩的世界古典史''. [[Changsha]]: Green Apple Data Center. [https://books.google.com/books?id=NletAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT237 p. 237].</ref> and ''[[sattva]]'' ({{Lang-sa|सत्त्व|label=none}}) of Indian thought.<ref>Curzer, H. J. 2012. ''Aristotle and the Virtues''. [[Oxford]]: [[Oxford University Press]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=z3d5IS4nbcUC&pg=PA77 p. 77].</ref><ref>Schadé, J. P., & [[Norbert Wiener|Wiener, N.]],
''Progress in Biocybernetics'' ([[Amsterdam]]: [[Elsevier]], 1966), p. 1.</ref>{{rp|1}}

==Ancient Greek literature==

In [[Ancient Greek literature]], sophrosyne is considered an important quality and is sometimes expressed in opposition to the concept of [[hubris]]. A noted example of this occurs in Homer's ''[[The Iliad]]''. When [[Agamemnon]] decides to take the queen [[Briseis]] away from [[Achilles]], it is seen as Agamemnon behaving with hubris and lacking sophrosyne.<ref name=":0">North, Helen. 1966. ''{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/sophrosyneselfkn00nort|title=Sophrosyne: Self-Knowledge and Self-Restraint in Greek Literature|url-access=registration}}'' (''Cornell Studies in Classical Philology'' 35). Ithaca: [[Cornell University Press]]. {{ISBN|9780801403187}}. {{JSTOR|10.7591/j.cttq4533}}.</ref> In Homer's ''[[Odyssey]]'', [[Odysseus]] avoids being turned by [[Circe]] the enchantress into an animal by means of a magical herb, [[Moly_(herb)|moly]] (symbolizing, by some accounts, sophrosyne), given to him by [[Athena]] (Wisdom) and [[Hermes]] (Reason).<ref>{{cite book|last=Clarke|first=Bruce|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=atMcSdSF67gC|title=Allegories of Writing: The Subject of Metamorphosis|publisher=[[SUNY Press]]|year=1995|isbn=9780791426234|location=[[Albany, New York|Albany]]}} p. 127.</ref>

[[Heraclitus]]'s fragment 112 states:<ref>[[Drew Hyland|Hyland, D. A.]] 2008. ''Plato and the Question of Beauty''. [[Bloomington, Indiana|Bloomington]]: [[Indiana University Press]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=C1LaszjLNawC&pg=PA105#v=onepage&q&f=false p. 105].</ref>

{{Verse translation|σωφρονεῖν ἀρετὴ μεγίστη, καὶ σοφίη ἀληθέα λέγειν καὶ ποιεῖν κατὰ φύσιν ἐπαίοντας|Sophrosyne is the greatest virtue, and wisdom is speaking and acting the truth, paying heed to the nature of things|italicsoff=yes}}

Themes connected with sophrosyne and hubris figure prominently in plays of [[Aeschylus]], [[Sophocles]] and [[Euripides]]; sophrosyne is recognized as a virtue, although debased forms like prudery are criticized.<ref>North, Helen F. 1947. "A Period of Opposition to Sôphrosynê in Greek Thought." ''[[Transactions of the American Philological Association|Transactions and Proceedings of the American Philological Association]]'' 78:1–17.</ref> Sophrosyne is a theme in the play ''[[Hippolytus (play)|Hippolytus]]'' by [[Euripides]], where sophrosyne is represented by the goddess [[Artemis]] and is personified by the character [[Hippolytus (son of Theseus)|Hippolytus]].<ref>Euripides. ''Hippolytus''. Bagg, Robert. Introduction. Oxford University Press. 1973 {{ISBN|978-0-19-507290-7}}.</ref><ref>[[Hazel Barnes|Barnes, H. E.]], "The Hippolytus of Drama and Myth", in D. Sutherland & H. E. Barnes, eds., ''Hippolytus in Drama and Myth'' 75, Nr. 2, [[Lincoln, Nebraska|Lincoln NE]], 1960, pp. 71–123.</ref>{{rp|71–123}}

===Goddess===
The 6th-century BC poet [[Theognis of Megara]] mentions Sophrosyne as among the [[Daemon (classical mythology)|daimona]] that were released from [[Pandora's box]].<ref>Rademaker, A. 2005. ''Sophrosyne and the Rhetoric of Self-Restraint: Polysemy & Persuasive Use of an Ancient Greek Value Term''. Leiden & Boston: [[Brill Publishers|Brill]]. pp. [https://books.google.cz/books?id=RSo_DwAAQBAJ&pg=PA76 76–78].</ref>

<blockquote><poem>[[Elpis (mythology)|Hope]] is the only good god remaining among mankind;
the others have left and gone to Olympus.
[[Pistis|Trust]], a mighty god has gone, Restraint (''Sophrosyne'') has gone from men,
and the [[Charites|Graces]], my friend, have abandoned the earth.</poem></blockquote>

The ''[[De Astronomica]]'' lists Continentia among the daughters of [[Erebus]] and [[Nyx]], who is thought to be the Roman equivalent of Sophrosyne.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Atsma |first1=Aaron J. |title=Sophrosyne |url=https://www.theoi.com/Daimon/Sophrosyne.html |website=Theoi Project |access-date=2019-05-28}}</ref>

===Plato===

Sophrosyne is an important topic for [[Plato]]. It is the main subject of the dialogue [[Charmides_(dialogue)|''Charmides'']], wherein several definitions are proposed but no conclusion reached; however the dramatic context connotes moral purity and innocence. An etymological meaning of sophrosyne as "moral sanity" is proposed in [[Cratylus_(dialogue) |''Cratylus'']] 411e. Plato's view of sophrosyne is related to Pythagorean ''harmonia'' ([[Republic_(Plato)|''Republic'']] 430e−432a, 442c) and closely linked with Plato’s 
[[Plato%27s tripartite theory of soul|tripartite division]] of the soul: ''sophrosyne'' is the harmonious moderation of the appetitive and spirited parts of the soul by the rational part (e.g., [[Phaedrus_(dialogue)|''Phaedrus'']] 237c−238e).<ref>{{cite book|first=Francis E.|last=Peters|author-link=Francis Edward Peters|title=Greek Philosophical Terms: A Historical Lexicon|page=[https://archive.org/details/greekphilosophic0000pete/page/179 179]|publisher=NYU Press|location=New York|year=1967|isbn=9780814765524}}</ref>

===After Plato===

For the Stoic, [[Zeno of Citium]], ''sophrosyne'' is one of the four chief virtues.<ref>[[Francis Edward Peters|Peters, F. E.]], ''Greek Philosophical Terms: A Historical Lexicon'' ([[New York City|New York]]: [[New York University Press|NYU Press]], 1967), [https://archive.org/details/greekphilosophic0000pete/page/180/mode/2up p. 180].</ref>{{rp|180}} Later Stoics like [[Musonius Rufus]], [[Seneca the younger|Seneca]], [[Epictetus]], and [[Marcus Aurelius]] took a practical view of ''sophrosyne'' and share a definition of it as the restraint of the appetites.<ref name=":0" />{{Rp|228-9}} 

Demophilus, a Pythagorean philosopher of uncertain date, wrote:<ref>''Sentences of Demophilus'' 39 = [[Stobaeus]], ''Florilegium'', vol. 1. Leipzig: [[Bibliotheca Teubneriana|Teubner]], 1855, p. 117 (Peri Sophrosyne 42).</ref>

{{Verse translation|Ρώμη ψυχής σωφροσύνη αύτη γαρ ψυχής απαθούς φώς εστιν|The vigor of the soul is sophrosyne, the light of a soul free of disturbing passions.|italicsoff=yes}}

[[Cicero]] considered four Latin terms to translate ''sophrosyne'': ''temperantia'' ([[Temperance_(virtue)|temperance]]), ''moderatio'' (moderateness), ''modestia'' (modesty) and ''frugalitas'' (frugality).<ref>Cicero, ''[[Tusculan Disputations]]'' 3.8.16.</ref> Through the writings of [[Lactantius]], [[Ambrose|St. Ambrose]] and [[Augustine|St. Augustine]], the virtue's meaning as temperance or "proper mixture" became the dominant view in subsequent Western European thought.<ref>{{cite book|first=M. F.|last=Carr|title=Passionate Deliberation: Emotion, Temperance, and the Care Ethic in Clinical Moral Deliberation|page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=7Z1yBgAAQBAJ&lpg=PP1&hl=de&pg=PA29 29]|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media|location=Berlin|year=2012|isbn=9789401005913}}</ref>

Sophrosyne, according to [[Thomas Aquinas|St. Thomas Aquinas]], is the fourth and final [[cardinal virtues|cardinal virtue]].<ref>{{cite book|last1=Harrington|first1=Daniel|last2=Keenan|first2=James|author1-link=Daniel J. Harrington|title=Paul and Virtue Ethics|date=2010|publisher=Rowman and Littlefield Publishers|location=Lanham, MD|pages=[https://books.google.com/books?id=y8golNYFEYgC&pg=PT125 125–126]}}</ref>{{rp|125–126}}

== See also ==
{{wiktionary|sophrosyne}}
* [[Seven virtues]]

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further reading ==
* North, Helen F. 1947. "[https://www.jstor.org/stable/283479 A period of opposition to sôphrosynê in Greek thought]." ''[[Transactions of the American Philological Association|Transactions and Proceedings of the American Philological Association]]'' 78:1–17.
* — 1966. ''Sophrosyne: Self-knowledge and self-restraint in Greek literature''. Ithaca, NY: [[Cornell University Press]].
* Rademaker, Adriaan. 2004. [https://books.google.com/books?id=RSo_DwAAQBAJ&printsec=frontcover ''Sophrosyne and the rhetoric of self-restraint: polysemy & persuasive use of an ancient Greek value term'']. Brill.
* Van Tongeren, Paul. 2001. "[https://repository.up.ac.za/bitstream/handle/2263/11494/VanTongeren_Nietzsche%282001%29.pdf?sequence=1 Nietzsche's revaluation of the cardinal virtues: The case of ''Sophrosyne'']." ''Phronimon: Journal of the South African Society for Greek Philosophy and the Humanities'' 3:128–49.

{{Plato navbox}}
{{Greek mythology (deities)}}

{{Authority control}}

[[Category:Ancient Greek philosophy]]
[[Category:Concepts in ancient Greek aesthetics]]
[[Category:Concepts in ancient Greek ethics]]
[[Category:Concepts in ancient Greek metaphysics]]
[[Category:Concepts in ancient Greek philosophy of mind]]
[[Category:Personifications in Greek mythology]]
[[Category:Religious ethics]]
[[Category:Virtue]]
[[Category:Words and phrases with no direct English translation]]
[[Category:Children of Nyx]]
[[Category:Greek goddesses]]
[[sn:Sophrosyne]]
[[Category:Pages]]
